_purpose: Local economy timeline for the NC Sandhills / Upper Cape Fear regional cluster (Moore, Harnett, Hoke, Scotland, Richmond, Anson, Montgomery counties). Covers 1740-1960 across six economic eras — from colonial Scots settlement through military-base transformation. Designed to give Polsia the economic backdrop for any ancestor living in this seven-county region so that narrative context sentences reflect what people actually did for a living, what they earned, and why they stayed or left.
display_name: NC Sandhills / Upper Cape Fear — Local Economy Timeline

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Naval stores (turpentine, tar, pitch) from longleaf pine forests; subsistence farming; Scottish Highland settler communities in the Cape Fear corridor
economic_eras.description: The Sandhills and Upper Cape Fear region was settled later than coastal NC, with Anson County (formed 1750 from Bladen) encompassing an enormous territory that would eventually be divided into most of the modern cluster. Scottish Highlanders, many recruited by the Crown after the 1745 Jacobite defeat, settled the upper Cape Fear corridor from the 1730s through the 1770s. These Gaelic-speaking families — MacNeills, McLeods, McLeans, Camerons, Stewarts — brought a clannish settlement pattern, establishing communities around kirk sessions and kin networks. Flora MacDonald herself lived near Red Springs (modern Scotland County) from 1774 to 1776. The longleaf pine forests that blanketed the sandy uplands supported a turpentine economy identical to the coastal plain — boxing pines, collecting crude gum, burning tar kilns. The deep Candor sand soils of the Sandhills proper (Moore, Hoke) were too poor for commercial agriculture, making naval stores the only viable cash economy. Corn, sweet potatoes, and free-range hogs provided subsistence. The Pee Dee River corridor through Anson and Richmond counties had richer alluvial soils that supported small-scale commercial farming, but the region as a whole was an economic backwater compared to the coastal tidewater. The Revolutionary War divided the Scottish community — most Highlanders remained Loyalist, fighting at the Battle of Moores Creek Bridge (February 1776) and suffering confiscation of property after the Patriot victory.

county_specifics.note: One of NC's oldest counties (formed 1750), covering a vast territory. The Pee Dee River bottomlands supported the largest farms. Wadesboro became the county seat. Early settlement included English, Scots-Irish, and Welsh families alongside the Highland Scots further west.

Formed from Anson in 1779. The Pee Dee corridor provided river access for modest commercial agriculture. Rockingham was established as county seat. Scottish Highland families dominated the western portions.

Formed from Anson in 1779. The Uwharrie Mountains made this county geologically distinct — rocky, hilly terrain with marginal farmland. Subsistence farming dominated. Gold would not be discovered until the 1790s.
Formed from Cumberland in 1784. Deep sand soils of the Sandhills dominated the eastern half. Turpentine was the primary cash economy. The western portion along the Deep River had better farmland.
Not yet formed (created 1855 from Cumberland). This territory was Cumberland County's northwestern corner, with Cape Fear River access supporting turpentine and subsistence farming.
Not yet formed (created 1899 from Richmond). This territory was the heart of the Scottish Highland settlement — the Argyll Colony centered around what would become Laurinburg.
Not yet formed (created 1911 from Robeson). This territory was deep Sandhills turpentine country in Robeson County's northwestern corner.

key_infrastructure.name: Cape Fear River navigation
key_infrastructure.impact: The Cape Fear provided the primary commercial outlet for the upper reaches of the region. Cross Creek (later Fayetteville) was the head of navigation and the gateway to Wilmington's port.

key_infrastructure.name: Scottish Highland settlement network
key_infrastructure.impact: Chain migration from Argyll and the western Highlands created kin-based communities that dominated the upper Cape Fear corridor. Kirk sessions and clan networks served as economic as well as social institutions.

key_infrastructure.name: Crown bounties on naval stores
key_infrastructure.impact: Parliamentary bounties made turpentine profitable on the poor Sandhills soils where no other cash crop was viable. Loss of the bounty after independence weakened the industry.

major_employers.name: Individual naval stores operators
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of small operations. Enslaved labor performed the most dangerous work — tar burning, gum collection in remote forests.

major_employers.name: Subsistence farms
major_employers.employment_scale: Dominant mode of labor — most families farmed their own or squatted land with minimal enslaved labor

wage_benchmarks.occupation: Free white tar burner / turpentine worker
wage_benchmarks.source: Merrens, Harry Roy. Colonial North Carolina in the Eighteenth Century. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1964, ch. 5.
Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 2004, ch. 2.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} settled in the upper Cape Fear country in the {decade}s, the longleaf pines stretched to the horizon over sand so deep it swallowed wagon wheels. The trees were the economy — boxed for turpentine, burned for tar — and the Gaelic-speaking neighbors who gathered at the kirk on Sunday had come from Argyll to work forests that looked nothing like the Highlands they left.
economic_eras.source: Meyer, Duane. The Highland Scots of North Carolina, 1732-1776. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1961. Merrens, Harry Roy. Colonial North Carolina in the Eighteenth Century. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1964. Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines: The Naval Stores Industry in the American South. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 2004.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Cotton on Pee Dee bottomlands (Anson, Richmond); turpentine in Sandhills longleaf pine (Moore, Hoke territory); gold mining in Uwharries (Montgomery); Plank Road era connecting interior to markets
economic_eras.description: The antebellum era split the cluster into three distinct economic zones. On the rich Pee Dee bottomlands of Anson County, cotton became king — Anson developed into one of the most plantation-intensive counties in the NC Piedmont, with large slaveholdings on Congaree silt loam that produced heavy yields. Richmond County's bottomlands supported a similar but less concentrated cotton economy. In the Sandhills proper (Moore, Hoke territory), the deep sand soils were useless for cotton, and turpentine remained the dominant cash economy into the 1860s. The longleaf pine forests of Moore County were boxed, drained, and distilled on an industrial scale. In the Uwharrie Mountains of Montgomery County, gold was discovered in the 1790s — part of the broader Carolina Gold Rush that preceded the 1849 California discovery. Mining operations generated court records, partnership agreements, and land claims that document families appearing in no other sources. The Plank Road era (1849-1860s) was transformative for the region: the Fayetteville & Western Plank Road and connecting routes provided the first reliable overland freight corridors, connecting the interior to Fayetteville's market. The 129-mile plank road to Salem was the longest in the world. Enslaved labor reached its peak across the cluster — Anson County alone had over 6,000 enslaved people by 1860, roughly half its population.

county_specifics.note: The cluster's cotton kingdom. Large plantations on Pee Dee bottomlands with slaveholdings exceeding 50 on the largest operations. Wadesboro was a prosperous courthouse town with merchants, lawyers, and a planter elite.
Cotton and mixed agriculture on the Pee Dee corridor. Rockingham grew as the county seat. Scottish Highland descendants maintained large landholdings.

The Carolina Gold Rush brought miners to the Uwharries in the 1790s-1840s. Reed Gold Mine (Cabarrus County) was the most famous, but Montgomery's mines — including the Iola and Russell mines — employed hundreds. Gold mining declined after California's 1849 discovery drained away skilled labor.
Turpentine dominated the eastern Sandhills portion. The western edge along the Deep River had better farmland. Carthage served as the county seat — a small courthouse village.

Formed from Cumberland in 1855. Cape Fear River access supported modest agriculture and some turpentine. Lillington became the county seat. The county's economy was less developed than older counties in the cluster.
Still part of Richmond County. The Scottish Highland families who had settled the area maintained large landholdings. Cotton and turpentine both important.
Still part of Robeson County. Deep Sandhills turpentine country. Among the most isolated and economically marginal areas in the cluster.

key_infrastructure.name: Fayetteville & Western Plank Road
key_infrastructure.impact: At 129 miles, the longest plank road in the world. Connected Fayetteville to Salem through Moore County and the Sandhills, finally giving the interior a reliable overland freight corridor. Toll gates every few miles charged 1-2 cents per mile for a loaded wagon.

key_infrastructure.name: Gold mines in Montgomery County
key_infrastructure.impact: The Uwharrie gold deposits drew prospectors, miners, and entrepreneurs. Mining claims, partnerships, and court disputes generated records. The industry peaked in the 1830s and declined sharply after 1849.

key_infrastructure.name: Cheraw & Darlington Railroad (to Richmond County)
key_infrastructure.impact: Early railroad connections from South Carolina reached the Pee Dee corridor, giving Richmond County farmers an alternative to the Cape Fear River route.

major_employers.name: Cotton plantations (Pee Dee corridor)
major_employers.employment_scale: Anson County had over 6,000 enslaved people by 1860. Richmond had over 4,000. Cotton production was the primary economic activity on the bottomlands.

major_employers.name: Turpentine operators (Sandhills)
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of operations ranging from single-operator farms to large turpentine plantations with dozens of enslaved workers.

major_employers.name: Gold mining operations (Uwharries)
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred miners at peak in the 1830s. Operations ranged from individual panning to organized mine shafts with ore-crushing equipment.

wage_benchmarks.source: Knapp, Richard F. Golden Promise in the Piedmont. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1975, ch. 3.
Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South. New York: Basic Books, 1986, ch. 1.
Wright, Old South, New South, ch. 1.
Outland, Tapping the Pines, ch. 4.
Starling, Robert B. 'The Plank Road Movement in North Carolina.' North Carolina Historical Review 16 (1939).

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By the {decade}s, the upper Cape Fear country had split into three worlds — the cotton plantations on the Pee Dee bottomlands where Anson County planters grew rich on enslaved labor, the turpentine forests of the Sandhills where {ancestor_name} boxed pines in sand too deep to plow, and the gold creeks of the Uwharries where a man could find enough color in a pan to make the backbreaking work worthwhile.
economic_eras.source: Meyer, The Highland Scots. Knapp, Richard F. Golden Promise in the Piedmont. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1975. Outland, Tapping the Pines. Starling, Robert B. 'The Plank Road Movement in North Carolina.' North Carolina Historical Review 16, nos. 1-2 (1939). Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South. New York: Basic Books, 1986.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Wartime collapse — troop movements, foraging armies, destruction of infrastructure, emancipation
economic_eras.description: The Civil War struck the Sandhills and Upper Cape Fear cluster with particular force in March 1865, when Sherman's army marched through the western portion of the region en route from Fayetteville to Goldsboro. The Fayetteville Arsenal was burned on March 11, 1865, and foraging parties swept through Harnett, Moore, and Cumberland counties, seizing livestock, emptying smokehouses, and burning fences. The Battle of Monroe's Crossroads (March 10, 1865, in Hoke territory) saw Confederate cavalry clash with Union forces near the modern Fort Bragg reservation. Anson County, further from Sherman's route, escaped the worst direct destruction but suffered from conscription, desertion, and the collapse of the cotton market. Montgomery County's isolation in the Uwharries provided some insulation, but the county contributed men to Confederate service and suffered the same economic disruption. The emancipation of thousands of enslaved people — over 6,000 in Anson County alone — eliminated the labor foundation of the cotton economy. The Scottish Highland communities in Richmond and Scotland territory split between Confederate and Union sympathies, with some families losing everything to property confiscation or wartime destruction.

county_specifics.note: Sherman's forces passed through the county's western edge. Foraging parties stripped farms. The turpentine economy was disrupted but the forests themselves were not destroyed.
Sherman's route from Fayetteville passed through the county. The Cape Fear River crossings were contested. Farms along the march route were stripped of all provisions.
Battle of Monroe's Crossroads (March 10, 1865) — Confederate cavalry attacked Union forces in what is now Fort Bragg's reservation. The surrounding farms were devastated by both armies.

Escaped direct military action but the economic collapse was comprehensive. Over 6,000 enslaved people were emancipated, destroying the plantation labor system. Cotton production fell by over 75% between 1860 and 1870.
Remote from Sherman's main route but suffered from conscription, desertion, and economic collapse. The Scottish community was divided in its loyalties.
The Uwharrie Mountains' isolation provided some protection from foraging armies, but deserter bands operated in the hills, and the county contributed heavily to Confederate service.
Part of Richmond County. Scottish descendants served in both armies. The economic damage was primarily from emancipation and the collapse of the cotton market rather than direct military action.

key_infrastructure.name: Burning of Fayetteville Arsenal
key_infrastructure.impact: Sherman burned the Confederate arsenal and armory on March 11, 1865. Fayetteville's primary industrial facility was destroyed, along with much of the town's commercial district. The effects rippled through Harnett and Moore counties, which depended on Fayetteville as their primary market.

key_infrastructure.name: Battle of Monroe's Crossroads
key_infrastructure.impact: Confederate cavalry attacked Union forces in Hoke territory (March 10, 1865). The battle disrupted the Sandhills region and left the surrounding farms devastated.

key_infrastructure.name: Plank Road destruction
key_infrastructure.impact: Sections of the plank road system were torn up by foraging armies and fell into disrepair. The plank road era effectively ended with the war.

major_employers.name: Confederate and Union armies
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of men under arms. The civilian economy was subordinated entirely to military needs.

wage_benchmarks.source: Confederate War Department records.
Escott, Paul D. Many Excellent People. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1985, ch. 4.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When Sherman's men came through the Sandhills in March 1865, they took what they could carry and burned what they couldn't. {ancestor_name}'s world — the plank road, the turpentine forest, the cotton field, the people who had worked the land — was broken in a single month.
economic_eras.source: Barrett, John G. The Civil War in North Carolina. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1963. Barrett, John G. Sherman's March Through the Carolinas. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1956. Escott, Paul D. Many Excellent People: Power and Privilege in North Carolina, 1850-1900. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1985.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Sharecropping and tenant farming (cotton in Anson/Richmond, tobacco and mixed crops elsewhere); turpentine in terminal decline; textile mills emerging in Harnett County; Hamlet railroad junction transforming Richmond County
economic_eras.description: The postwar economy reorganized around sharecropping on the Pee Dee bottomlands, where former plantations were divided into tenant plots. Anson County's cotton economy continued under the new labor system, but the crop lien trapped both Black and white families in debt cycles. The turpentine industry entered its final decline in the Sandhills as longleaf pine forests were exhausted — the industry's center shifted to South Carolina and Florida. The transformative development of this era was the railroad. The Raleigh & Augusta Air Line Railroad reached Hamlet (Richmond County) in 1874, and the Seaboard Air Line Railroad made Hamlet a major junction by 1892 — where the north-south corridor from Norfolk to Atlanta intersected the east-west route from Raleigh to Charlotte. Hamlet's population exploded from nothing to over 3,000 by 1900, and the railroad shops and yards became the largest non-agricultural employer in the cluster. In Harnett County, the Erwin Cotton Mills Company (controlled by the Duke interests) established a massive textile operation at Erwin in 1893, creating a company town that drew families from failing farms across the region. The Dunn Cotton Manufacturing Company followed. These mills represented the first wave of the farm-to-mill pipeline in this cluster. Scotland County was carved from Richmond in 1899, creating a new county at Laurinburg — the culmination of the Scottish Highland community's desire for self-governance after 120 years as part of a larger county.

county_specifics.note: The cotton economy continued under sharecropping. Soil exhaustion from decades of monoculture cotton was already visible. Wadesboro's merchant class maintained the crop lien system. Black landownership grew slowly in the decades after emancipation.

The Hamlet railroad junction (1874-1892) transformed the county's economy. The Seaboard Air Line Railroad shops employed hundreds. Rockingham remained the county seat, but Hamlet became the economic engine.

Formed from Richmond in 1899. Laurinburg became the county seat. The Scottish Highland families maintained large landholdings. Cotton and turpentine both important. Laurinburg Institute, a private school for Black students, was founded in 1904.

Gold mining was effectively dead by the 1860s. The county reverted to subsistence farming and timber. The Uwharries' poor soils and isolation made it the least economically developed county in the cluster.

Turpentine declined as forests were cut out. Timber replaced turpentine as the primary extractive industry. In 1895, James Tufts founded the Pinehurst resort, importing a New England resort model into the Sandhills — the beginning of a transformation that would eventually make tourism Moore County's primary industry.

The Erwin Cotton Mills (1893) and Dunn cotton mills represented the farm-to-mill pipeline arriving in the cluster. Company towns at Erwin and Dunn drew families from farms across the region. The Cape Fear River provided waterpower.
Still part of Robeson County. Turpentine in decline. Among the most impoverished and isolated areas in the region. Would not become its own county until 1911.

key_infrastructure.name: Hamlet railroad junction
key_infrastructure.impact: The intersection of the Raleigh & Augusta Air Line and the Seaboard Air Line railroads made Hamlet the most important railroad junction between Raleigh and Atlanta. Railroad shops, roundhouses, and yards employed hundreds. Hamlet grew from nothing to a railroad town of 3,000 in two decades.

key_infrastructure.name: Erwin Cotton Mills
key_infrastructure.impact: The Duke-controlled textile mill at Erwin (Harnett County) was one of the largest in eastern NC. The company town included mill housing, a company store, churches, and schools — a self-contained community that drew families from failing farms. The mill produced denim and work fabrics.

key_infrastructure.name: Pinehurst resort
key_infrastructure.impact: James Tufts of Boston purchased 5,800 acres of cutover pineland in Moore County and built a winter resort for Northern health-seekers. By 1900, Pinehurst had a hotel, cottages, and the first golf courses in the Sandhills. The resort economy would eventually transform Moore County from turpentine backwater to tourism destination.

key_infrastructure.name: Scotland County formation
key_infrastructure.impact: The Scottish Highland community around Laurinburg achieved county independence from Richmond County. The new county's identity was explicitly tied to its Scottish heritage.

major_employers.name: Sharecropping farms (cotton, mixed crops)
major_employers.employment_scale: Dominant employment mode for both Black and white families across the cluster

major_employers.name: Seaboard Air Line Railroad (Hamlet)
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred permanent employees in shops and yards; additional section gangs throughout the region. Largest non-agricultural employer in the cluster.

major_employers.name: Erwin Cotton Mills
major_employers.employment_scale: Over 1,000 employees by 1910. Drew labor from farms across the cluster and from the SE NC Coastal Plain.

major_employers.name: Pinehurst resort
major_employers.employment_scale: Seasonal — several hundred during winter resort season. Black workers served as caddies, hotel staff, and groundskeepers; white workers in skilled trades and management.

wage_benchmarks.source: Ransom and Sutch. One Kind of Freedom. 2nd ed. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2001, chs. 4-6.
Seaboard Air Line Railroad historical records; NC Bureau of Labor Statistics.
Hall, Jacquelyn Dowd, et al. Like a Family. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1987, ch. 2.
Tufts Archives, Pinehurst; Moore County Historical Association.
Wright, Old South, New South, ch. 3.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: After the war, the Sandhills country split into new worlds — {ancestor_name} could work cotton on shares in Anson County, box turpentine in the dying pine forests of Moore, take a railroad job at Hamlet junction, or walk through the mill gate at Erwin where the Duke interests were hiring anyone who could tend a loom.
economic_eras.source: Ransom, Roger L., and Richard Sutch. One Kind of Freedom. 2nd ed. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2001. Hall, Jacquelyn Dowd, et al. Like a Family: The Making of a Southern Cotton Mill World. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1987. Meyer, The Highland Scots. Wright, Old South, New South.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Three parallel economies — Pinehurst/Southern Pines resort colony (Moore); textile mills (Harnett); railroad junction (Richmond/Hamlet) — while cotton declines in Anson/Richmond and subsistence farming persists in Montgomery
economic_eras.description: Between 1900 and 1940, the cluster's economy fractured into three distinct and largely self-contained worlds. In Moore County, the Pinehurst resort expanded into a winter colony for wealthy Northerners — golf courses, fox hunting, and equestrian events created a class enclave utterly unlike anything else in the Sandhills. Southern Pines developed as a tuberculosis sanatorium town, drawing health-seekers to the dry pine air. These resort communities employed a large Black service workforce — caddies, hotel staff, domestic workers — at wages marginally above farm labor but within a rigid racial hierarchy. In Harnett County, the Erwin Cotton Mills expanded steadily, and additional mills opened at Dunn and Lillington. The company town model reached its peak: mill housing, company stores, mill churches, and mill schools created total communities that families entered and rarely left. The farm-to-mill pipeline drew families from failing farms across the cluster and from the SE NC Coastal Plain. In Richmond County, the Hamlet railroad junction reached its peak importance as the Seaboard Air Line became a major through-route from the Northeast to Florida. Railroad employment was the best-paid blue-collar work in the cluster, and Hamlet's railroad families formed a distinct working-class community with higher wages and greater stability than either farm or mill families. Cotton continued to decline in Anson and Richmond counties as soil exhaustion and the boll weevil (arriving in the 1920s) undermined the Pee Dee bottomland economy. Montgomery County remained isolated and subsistence-oriented, with timber the only significant cash economy.

county_specifics.note: Pinehurst and Southern Pines created a dual economy — wealthy winter residents coexisting with local farm and service families. The resort's economic influence expanded throughout the period. Aberdeen, on the railroad, served as the commercial gateway.

Erwin was a full company town by 1910 — the mill employed over 1,500 people and the company controlled housing, retail, and social life. Dunn and Lillington developed smaller textile operations. The Cape Fear River provided waterpower.

Hamlet's railroad economy peaked. The Seaboard Air Line shops employed over 500 workers. Rockingham developed a small textile industry of its own. The county's economy was the most diversified in the cluster.

Cotton farming continued on Scottish family lands. Laurinburg grew modestly. The Laurinburg Institute provided education for Black students. The economy remained agricultural with no significant industrial employer.

Cotton declined as soils were exhausted and the boll weevil arrived in the 1920s. Wadesboro lost relative importance. Textile mills appeared in small towns — Anson County's Wadesboro had small mill operations — but nothing approaching Erwin's scale.
Timber extraction — sawmills cutting the remaining hardwood forests of the Uwharries — was the primary cash economy. Farming remained marginal. Troy was the county seat but a small, isolated village.

Formed from Robeson in 1911 — NC's youngest county. Raeford became the county seat. Turpentine was effectively dead. Cotton and subsistence farming dominated. The county had no significant industrial employer.

key_infrastructure.name: Pinehurst golf courses (Donald Ross)
key_infrastructure.impact: Donald Ross, a Scottish-born golf course architect, designed Pinehurst No. 2 (1907) and other courses that made Moore County a golfing destination of national prominence. The courses employed dozens of Black caddies and groundskeepers year-round.

key_infrastructure.name: Southern Pines sanatorium district
key_infrastructure.impact: The dry pine air attracted tuberculosis patients. Multiple sanatoriums operated in Southern Pines, creating a medical economy alongside the resort economy. The sanatorium era ended when effective TB treatments became available in the 1940s.

key_infrastructure.name: Seaboard Air Line through-route to Florida
key_infrastructure.impact: The SAL's premier trains — including the Silver Meteor and Orange Blossom Special — passed through Hamlet, making it a nationally significant junction. Railroad employment peaked at over 500 workers.

key_infrastructure.name: Hoke County formation
key_infrastructure.impact: NC's youngest county. The formation reflected the desire of northwestern Robeson County communities for a county seat closer than Lumberton. Raeford was selected as seat.

key_infrastructure.name: Boll weevil arrival
key_infrastructure.impact: The cotton boll weevil reached the Pee Dee corridor in the early 1920s, devastating Anson and Richmond counties' cotton production. Yields dropped sharply. Some farmers diversified into tobacco, peaches, or livestock.

major_employers.name: Erwin Cotton Mills (and subsidiary plants)
major_employers.employment_scale: Over 1,500 workers by 1920. The single largest employer in the cluster. Drew labor from a 50-mile radius.

major_employers.name: Seaboard Air Line Railroad (Hamlet)
major_employers.employment_scale: 500+ workers in shops and yards. Best-paid blue-collar work in the cluster.

major_employers.name: Pinehurst/Southern Pines resort complex
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred seasonal workers; smaller year-round workforce. Black service workers constituted the majority of resort labor.

major_employers.name: Cotton and mixed farms (sharecropping)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining but still the primary livelihood for most rural families

major_employers.name: Sawmills and timber operations (Uwharries)
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred seasonal workers across multiple small mills

wage_benchmarks.source: Hall et al., Like a Family, ch. 4; NC Bureau of Labor Statistics Annual Report, 1920.
Seaboard Air Line Railroad payroll records; NC Bureau of Labor Statistics.
Tufts Archives, Pinehurst.
Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985, ch. 3.
Wright, Old South, New South, ch. 4.
NC Bureau of Labor Statistics Annual Report, 1920.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By the {decade}s, {ancestor_name} could see three different futures in the Sandhills — the resort world at Pinehurst where wealthy Northerners played golf on courses kept by Black caddies who lived in a different century, the mill gates at Erwin where the Duke interests hired anyone willing to tend looms for a dollar a day, and the railroad yards at Hamlet where a man with skills could earn enough to own a house.
economic_eras.source: Hall et al., Like a Family. Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985. Wright, Old South, New South. Tufts Archives, Pinehurst. Moore County Historical Association records.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Fort Bragg and Camp Mackall military expansion dominates western half of cluster; textile mills persist in Harnett; cotton under allotments in Anson/Richmond; mechanization displacing farm labor everywhere
economic_eras.description: World War II transformed the NC Sandhills more profoundly than any event since the original Scottish settlement. Fort Bragg, established in 1918 as a WWI artillery training camp in Cumberland County, expanded massively in 1940-1942 into the largest military installation in the world by population. The reservation extended deep into Hoke County and Moore County's eastern edge, swallowing farms and displacing families. Camp Mackall, built in 1942 as an airborne training center in Richmond and Moore counties, added another military footprint. The military payroll and the service economy it generated — housing, restaurants, laundry, entertainment — transformed the western half of the cluster from agricultural backwater to military-service economy. Fayetteville (in adjacent Cumberland County) boomed, and the effects rippled outward into Harnett, Moore, and Hoke counties. In the eastern half of the cluster, the old agricultural economy continued its decline. Federal tobacco and cotton allotments (post-1933) stabilized prices but accelerated the displacement of tenant families. Mechanization — tractors replacing mules, mechanical cotton pickers — reduced labor demand. The farm-to-mill pipeline continued at Erwin and Dunn in Harnett County, but the mills themselves were beginning to face competition from synthetic fibers and foreign producers. Anson County's economy stagnated as cotton declined and no replacement employer emerged. Montgomery County remained the cluster's poorest county, with timber and marginal farming the only options until the federal government designated the Uwharrie National Forest in 1961, providing a modest economic floor through forest service employment and conservation programs. By 1960, the cluster's economy had bifurcated: the military-connected western counties (Moore, Hoke, Harnett) were growing, while the agricultural eastern counties (Anson, Montgomery, Richmond, Scotland) were losing population to out-migration.

county_specifics.note: Fort Bragg's eastern boundary and Camp Mackall's presence brought military economic influence. Pinehurst and Southern Pines continued to grow as resort communities. The county's economy was the most diversified in the cluster — military, resort, agriculture, and small-scale manufacturing.

Fort Bragg's northern boundary extended into Harnett. Military commuters supplemented the textile economy. Erwin Cotton Mills remained a major employer but began to face competitive pressure. Dunn and Lillington grew modestly.

Fort Bragg dominated the northern half of the county. Farm families displaced by military land acquisition received compensation but lost ancestral lands. Raeford's economy reoriented toward serving the military installation. Turkey farming emerged as a new agricultural sector.

Camp Mackall was built partly in Richmond County. The Hamlet railroad junction remained important but began to decline as highway freight displaced rail. Rockingham's textile industry provided some diversification.
No military installation. Agriculture continued its decline. Laurinburg grew slowly. The county's economy was among the least diversified in the cluster.
Cotton under allotments. No military or industrial replacement for the declining agricultural economy. Population loss through out-migration was significant. Wadesboro stagnated.
The poorest county in the cluster. Timber and subsistence farming. The Uwharrie National Forest designation (1961) provided modest federal employment. Troy remained a small, isolated county seat.

key_infrastructure.name: Fort Bragg WWII expansion
key_infrastructure.impact: Fort Bragg expanded from a peacetime garrison into the world's largest military installation by population. Construction employed tens of thousands. The permanent military population and civilian workforce created a service economy that extended throughout the western cluster. Land acquisition displaced hundreds of farm families in Hoke and Cumberland counties.

key_infrastructure.name: Camp Mackall
key_infrastructure.impact: Built as an airborne and glider training center in Richmond and Moore counties. Named for Pvt. John Mackall, killed at Buna. The 62,000-acre reservation absorbed farmland and created construction jobs. After WWII, the camp was deactivated but the land remained federal property, used for periodic training.

key_infrastructure.name: Agricultural Adjustment Act — cotton and tobacco allotments
key_infrastructure.impact: Federal allotments stabilized crop prices but reduced acreage and accelerated the displacement of tenant families. Allotments were assigned to landowners, giving them leverage to consolidate holdings and reduce tenants.

key_infrastructure.name: Uwharrie National Forest designation
key_infrastructure.impact: Federal designation of the Uwharrie National Forest in Montgomery County provided conservation employment and recreational development on lands too marginal for profitable farming. The designation preserved the Uwharrie landscape while providing a modest economic floor.

key_infrastructure.name: Rural electrification (REA)
key_infrastructure.impact: The REA brought electric power to farm communities across the cluster. Electric motors, refrigeration, and radio transformed daily life. Rural electrification was slower in the Sandhills and Uwharries than in the Piedmont due to dispersed population.

major_employers.name: Fort Bragg (U.S. Army)
major_employers.employment_scale: Peak WWII military population of 159,000; postwar garrison of 30,000-50,000 plus thousands of civilian employees. Dominant employer in the western cluster.

major_employers.name: Camp Mackall (U.S. Army)
major_employers.employment_scale: Peak wartime population of approximately 8,000. Deactivated after WWII but retained for periodic training.

major_employers.name: Erwin Cotton Mills / Burlington Industries
major_employers.employment_scale: Over 1,500 workers. Acquired by Burlington Industries in the 1950s. Still a major employer but facing competitive pressure.

major_employers.name: Seaboard Air Line Railroad (Hamlet)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining — diesel locomotives replaced steam, requiring fewer shop workers. Employment dropped from over 500 to under 300 by 1960.

major_employers.name: Pinehurst/Southern Pines resort
major_employers.employment_scale: Growing — year-round operation expanded as golf tourism increased. Several hundred permanent and seasonal workers.

wage_benchmarks.source: U.S. Army payroll records; Fayetteville Observer historical archives.
Hall et al., Like a Family, ch. 6; NC Department of Labor.
Seaboard Air Line Railroad payroll records.
Daniel, Breaking the Land, ch. 5.
USDA Agricultural Statistics; NC Employment Security Commission.
Tufts Archives, Pinehurst.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When Fort Bragg swallowed the farms in northern Hoke County, {ancestor_name} watched the Sandhills change from turpentine country to military country in a single generation. The Army paid every month regardless of the rain or the boll weevil — and that kind of money pulled people off the land faster than a hundred years of soil exhaustion had managed to do.
economic_eras.source: Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985. Hall et al., Like a Family. Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1964. U.S. Army Center of Military History, Fort Bragg installation history.

economic_turning_points.event: Battle of Moores Creek Bridge — Loyalist Scots defeated
economic_turning_points.impact: The defeat of Highland Scots Loyalists at Moores Creek Bridge (February 27, 1776) was the defining political event of the colonial era for this cluster. Property confiscation followed — Loyalist estates were seized and redistributed. The Scottish Highland community that had dominated the upper Cape Fear corridor was shattered. Patriot families received confiscated land; Loyalist families lost everything. The economic consequences persisted for decades as former Loyalist families struggled to rebuild.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name}'s Highland family chose the wrong side at Moores Creek in 1776, the confiscation that followed could explain why the land records start over — because the Crown's promise to the MacNeills and Camerons died with the Loyalist cause.
economic_turning_points.source: Meyer, Duane. The Highland Scots of North Carolina, 1732-1776. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1961.

economic_turning_points.impact: The world's longest plank road connected the Sandhills interior to Fayetteville's markets for the first time by a reliable overland route. Before the plank road, farms more than a day's wagon ride from the Cape Fear River existed in near-total commercial isolation. The plank road collapsed travel time and opened the interior to market agriculture — but it also charged tolls that consumed a significant portion of small farmers' profits. The plank road era lasted barely fifteen years before the Civil War destroyed the system.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The plank road through {county} County changed what {ancestor_name} could sell — before it, the turpentine barrels rotted waiting for a dry spell to haul them. After, a loaded wagon could make Fayetteville in a day, tolls and all.
economic_turning_points.source: Starling, Robert B. 'The Plank Road Movement in North Carolina.' North Carolina Historical Review 16, nos. 1-2 (1939).

economic_turning_points.event: Hamlet becomes Seaboard Air Line Railroad junction
economic_turning_points.impact: The intersection of the SAL's north-south and east-west routes at Hamlet created the cluster's first true industrial employer. Railroad shops, roundhouses, and yards employed several hundred workers at wages far above farming. Hamlet grew from nothing to a substantial town in two decades, and the railroad payroll circulated cash through an economy that had previously operated almost entirely on credit and barter.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The railroad yard at Hamlet was the first place in the Sandhills where {ancestor_name} could earn cash wages every week — not seasonal, not dependent on the crop price, not paid in store credit. The trains ran regardless of the weather, and the machinists got paid regardless of the harvest.
economic_turning_points.source: Seaboard Air Line Railroad historical records. NC Bureau of Labor Statistics.

economic_turning_points.event: Erwin Cotton Mills opens in Harnett County
economic_turning_points.impact: The Duke-controlled textile mill at Erwin created the cluster's first company town and the largest single employer. The mill drew families from failing farms across the Sandhills and Upper Cape Fear, channeling them into the farm-to-mill pipeline that had already transformed the Piedmont. By 1910, Erwin employed over 1,500 workers — more than the combined non-agricultural workforce of any two other counties in the cluster.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} walked through the mill gate at Erwin for the first time, {pronoun_subject} left the farming world behind — and entered a company town where the Duke interests owned the house, the store, the church, and the paycheck.
economic_turning_points.source: Hall, Jacquelyn Dowd, et al. Like a Family. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 1987, chs. 1-3.

economic_turning_points.event: Fort Bragg WWII expansion and Camp Mackall construction
economic_turning_points.impact: The simultaneous massive expansion of Fort Bragg and the construction of Camp Mackall transformed the western half of the cluster from agricultural backwater to military-service economy. For the first time since the colonial naval stores era, the Sandhills had a major non-agricultural economic engine. The military payroll exceeded the combined value of all agricultural production in the affected counties by 1943. Land acquisition displaced hundreds of farm families, ending multi-generational land tenure.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: When the Army took {ancestor_name}'s farm in northern Hoke County for Fort Bragg's expansion in {year}, the condemnation file was the last property record the family would ever generate on that land — and the first record in a new story that had nothing to do with farming.
economic_turning_points.source: Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1964. U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Fort Bragg installation history.

economic_turning_points.impact: James Tufts' purchase of 5,800 acres of cutover Sandhills pineland and the creation of a winter resort introduced a wholly new economic model to the region. Over the following decades, Pinehurst and Southern Pines became nationally prominent resort communities, employing hundreds of local workers (primarily Black service staff) and drawing wealthy Northern visitors who spent more money in a single winter season than most local farms earned in a year. The resort economy created a stark class divide visible in the landscape — manicured golf courses adjacent to sharecropper cabins.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: Pinehurst was a different country planted in the middle of {county} County — {ancestor_name} could see it from the road, the Northern visitors playing golf on grass greener than anything the sandy land would grow for a farmer, while the caddies who carried their bags went home to the same cabins the turpentine workers had left behind.
economic_turning_points.source: Tufts Archives, Pinehurst. Moore County Historical Association.

narrative_hooks: In {county} County in the {decade}s, the economy ran on {commodity} — and every family's year was measured by the price it brought at {market_town}.
When {ancestor_name} looked for work in the Sandhills around {year}, the options were the farm, the mill, the railroad, and leaving — and each choice led to a different world within twenty miles.
The railroad junction at Hamlet paid cash wages every week — a revolution in a region where {ancestor_name}'s family had never seen money that wasn't attached to a crop lien note.

The turpentine forests that had sustained {ancestor_name}'s grandparents were stumps by the {decade}s — and the sand beneath them was too poor to plow, leaving nothing but the mill gate or the military base.

Anson County's cotton kingdom on the Pee Dee bottoms ran on enslaved labor until 1865 and sharecropper labor after — {ancestor_name}'s place in that system determined everything about the records that survive.
The gold creeks of the Uwharries drew {ancestor_name} to Montgomery County — a place where a man could pan enough color to eat, in mountains too old and worn down to grow anything worth selling.
Fort Bragg changed everything in the western Sandhills — {ancestor_name} watched farm roads become base roads and turpentine country become artillery ranges.
When {ancestor_name} walked through the mill gate at Erwin in {year}, {pronoun_subject} joined a community where the Duke interests owned the house, the store, and the future.
source_references: Meyer, Duane. The Highland Scots of North Carolina, 1732-1776. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1961.
Knapp, Richard F. Golden Promise in the Piedmont: The Story of John Reed's Mine. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1975.
Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines: The Naval Stores Industry in the American South. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 2004.

Hall, Jacquelyn Dowd, James Leloudis, Robert Korstad, Mary Murphy, Lu Ann Jones, and Christopher B. Daly. Like a Family: The Making of a Southern Cotton Mill World. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1987.
Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985.
Ransom, Roger L., and Richard Sutch. One Kind of Freedom: The Economic Consequences of Emancipation. 2nd ed. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2001.
Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South: Revolutions in the Southern Economy since the Civil War. New York: Basic Books, 1986.
Barrett, John G. The Civil War in North Carolina. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1963.
Escott, Paul D. Many Excellent People: Power and Privilege in North Carolina, 1850-1900. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1985.
Starling, Robert B. 'The Plank Road Movement in North Carolina.' North Carolina Historical Review 16, nos. 1-2 (1939).
Lemmon, Sarah McCulloh. North Carolina's Role in World War II. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1964.
